name: Link-Time Optimisation — Full LTO and ThinLTO
summary: LTO defers part of compilation until link time. Full LTO merges all bitcode into one giant module — maximum optimisation, serial bottleneck. ThinLTO (Tobias Grosser, Teresa Johnson 2016) decouples 'decide what to inline' from 'apply' and parallelises the latter. ThinLTO is the production default for serious C++ at scale.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**Link-time optimisation (LTO)** defers part of compilation until link time, when the whole program (or module set) is available. The canonical wins are cross-module inlining, whole-program dead-code elimination, and devirtualisation across translation unit boundaries.

**Full LTO** (GCC's `-flto`, Clang's `-flto=full`) merges all LLVM IR bitcode from every translation unit into one giant module, then runs the full optimiser on it. Maximum code quality; minimal scalability — the link step becomes an O(program size) serial bottleneck, often adding minutes to link times on large projects.

**ThinLTO** (Tobias Grosser, Teresa Johnson et al., 2016) is Clang's scalable alternative. Each translation unit emits bitcode with a summary of what's exported, imported, and called. The linker reads all summaries, builds a call graph, decides per-function what to inline across modules, and *then* parallelises the actual optimisation across the translation units. Link time approaches that of non-LTO builds while retaining most of Full LTO's benefits.

sections.title: Design insight — separate decision from application
sections.content:
ThinLTO's key design insight: cross-module information flow at link time doesn't require serial processing, if you separate "decide what to do" from "do it" and parallelise the second phase.

The lesson generalises to any whole-program analysis: build summaries per module that admit decisions, make decisions globally with the summaries, then parallelise the per-module application of those decisions. Every serious C/C++ codebase at scale uses ThinLTO today; Full LTO is reserved for small, performance-critical components.
